     "gif": ("pillow", "gif", {"fps": 10}, []),
     "scrubber": ("html", None, {"fps": 5}, None),
@@ -152,6 +150,8 @@ def _figure_data(self, plot, fmt, bbox_inches="tight", as_script=False, **kwargs
         """
         if fmt in ["gif", "mp4", "webm"]:
             with mpl.rc_context(rc=plot.fig_rcparams):
+                if bbox_inches == "tight":
+                    self._adjust_figure_for_anim(plot, fmt)
                 anim = plot.anim(fps=self.fps)
             data = self._anim_data(anim, fmt)
         else:
@@ -235,6 +235,78 @@ def _compute_bbox(self, fig, kw):
                 kw["bbox_inches"] = MPLRenderer.drawn[fig_id]
         return kw
 
+    def _adjust_figure_for_anim(self, plot, fmt):
+        """Adjust figure size and subplot positions to tightly fit all content.
+
+        Matplotlib's animation writers do not support bbox_inches='tight',
+        so animations can have clipped labels and titles. This method
+        computes the tight bounding box of the rendered figure and adjusts
+        the figure dimensions and axes positions so that all content fits
+        within the figure bounds without clipping.
+        """
+        from matplotlib.backends.backend_agg import FigureCanvasAgg
+
+        fig = plot.state
+
+        traverse_fn = lambda x: x.handles.get("bbox_extra_artists", None)
+        extra_artists = list(
+            chain.from_iterable(
+                artists for artists in plot.traverse(traverse_fn) if artists is not None
+            )
+        )
+
+        pad = mpl.rcParams["savefig.pad_inches"]
+
+        # Ensure we have a non-interactive canvas for rendering, since
+        # the figure may have been closed (plt.close) but still retain
+        # a Tk/Qt canvas that errors on resize operations.
+        if type(fig.canvas) is not FigureCanvasAgg:
+            fig.canvas.manager = None
+            FigureCanvasAgg(fig)
+
+        dpi = self.dpi or fig.dpi
+        fig.set_dpi(dpi)
+        fig.canvas.draw()
+        bbox_inches = get_tight_bbox(fig, extra_artists, pad=pad)
+
+        orig_w, orig_h = fig.get_size_inches()
+        new_w = bbox_inches.width
+        new_h = bbox_inches.height
+
+        # Video codecs like libx264 require even pixel dimensions.
+        # Round up to the nearest even number of pixels for video formats.
+        if fmt in ("mp4", "webm"):
+            pw, ph = int(new_w * dpi), int(new_h * dpi)
+            new_w = (pw + pw % 2) / dpi
+            new_h = (ph + ph % 2) / dpi
+
+        # Compute how to transform positions from old figure coordinates
+        # to new figure coordinates. The tight bbox defines the visible
+        # region in the old coordinate system; we need to map that region
+        # to fill the new (resized) figure.
+        x_shift = -bbox_inches.x0 / orig_w
+        y_shift = -bbox_inches.y0 / orig_h
+        x_scale = orig_w / new_w
+        y_scale = orig_h / new_h
+
+        for ax in fig.axes:
+            pos = ax.get_position()
+            new_pos = [
+                (pos.x0 + x_shift) * x_scale,
+                (pos.y0 + y_shift) * y_scale,
+                pos.width * x_scale,
+                pos.height * y_scale,
+            ]
+            ax.set_position(new_pos)
+
+        # Reposition figure-level texts (e.g. suptitle) which use
+        # figure-fraction coordinates and are not moved by axes adjustment.
+        for text in fig.texts:
+            x, y = text.get_position()
+            text.set_position(((x + x_shift) * x_scale, (y + y_shift) * y_scale))
+
+        fig.set_size_inches(new_w, new_h)
+
     @classmethod
     @contextmanager
     def state(cls):

problem_statement:
Matplotlib animations are clipped



#### ALL software version info
holoviews 1.14.4 pyhd3eb1b0_1
matplotlib 3.3.4 py39h06a4308_0
python 3.9.5 h12debd9_4
Ubuntu 20.04.2 LTS


#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior
When plotting an animation with the matplotlib backend, the edges of the figure are clipped. This happens for both gif and webm animations. When saving the same figure to a static figure such as png, the edges are not clipped.

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
import holoviews as hv
import numpy as np

hv.extension("matplotlib")

frequencies = np.linspace(0.5, 0.6, 5)

def sine_curve(phase, freq):
    xvals = [0.1* i for i in range(100)]
    return hv.Curve((xvals, [np.sin(phase+freq*x) for x in xvals]))

curve_dict = {f:sine_curve(0,f) for f in frequencies}
hmap = hv.HoloMap(curve_dict, kdims='frequency')

hv.save(hmap, 'sines.webm', fmt='webm')
hv.save(hmap, 'sines.gif', fmt='gif', fps=5)
hv.save(hmap, 'sines.png', fmt='png')

layout = hmap + hmap
hv.save(layout, 'sines_layout.webm', fmt='webm')
hv.save(layout, 'sines_layout.gif', fmt='gif', fps=5)
hv.save(layout, 'sines_layout.png', fmt='png')

```


#### Screenshots or screencasts of the bug in action
`sines.png` file showing no clipping
![sines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122196717-6fdadb00-ced2-11eb-99b1-a91b9fac79b5.png)
`sines.gif` files showing clipping of the y-label, and a wide right margin
![sines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122196709-6ea9ae00-ced2-11eb-808d-bb9cf892b38b.gif)

Same as above for a layout. Here the clipping is even more clear, affecting the title in addition to the left and lower margins
![sines_layout](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122197039-c2b49280-ced2-11eb-82e3-ca28845d0dc7.png)

![sines_layout](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122197043-c34d2900-ced2-11eb-8162-1c1945eca986.gif)
hints_text:
I did a bit of digging, and saving the first frame of the animation 
```
anim = hv.render(layout)
fig = anim._fig
anim._fig.savefig("sines0.png")
```
with `layout` from the above code also gives a clipped image
![sines0](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122199474-090af100-ced5-11eb-83f3-f3180db01663.png)

So it does not appear that the problem is in the animation stage, but rather in each frame figure. Maybe the axes are being put in different positions in an animation compared to a static image export?
I found a workaround for this issue, by manually tuning the `fig_bounds` option:
```
layout.opts(fig_bounds=(0.1, 0.1, 0.95, 0.9))

anim = hv.render(layout)
anim._fig.savefig("sines_fig_bounds.png")
```
with `layout` from the first post in this thread. This gives
![sines_fig_bounds](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122511688-99b30f80-d042-11eb-9d1d-34f4d4a80493.png)

In this plot the title is still cropped, but this can be worked around with a hook
```
def move_title_hook(plot, elem):
    fig = plot.state
    text = fig._suptitle
    if text:
        text.set_position((0.5, 0.97))

layout.opts(hv.opts.Curve(hooks=[move_title_hook]))

anim = hv.render(layout)
anim._fig.savefig("sines_title_moved.png")
```
giving
![sines_title_moved](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37403847/122511794-c8c98100-d042-11eb-8d34-d130b77d80e7.png)

The underlying cause for this problem seems to be related to what's going on in the `_figure_data` method of the `holoviews.plotting.mpl.renderer.MPLRenderer` class
 https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/blob/c425b5546adeb55bef5c34ed08343b69df0ca35b/holoviews/plotting/mpl/renderer.py#L135
For static image export a bounding box containing all the artists is calculated using the `_compute_bbox` method, and this box ensures that no part of the figure is clipped. This bounding box is not calculated for an animation.

I tried manually calculating a bounding box, and then pass it into the `savefig_kwargs` in `anim_kwargs` in the `anim.save` call in the `_anim_data` method of the `MPLRenderer`, but then matplotlib simply ignores this bounding box, with the following warning
```
Warning: discarding the 'bbox_inches' argument in 'savefig_kwargs' as it may cause frame size to vary, which is inappropriate for animation.
```

This leads to me think that maybe the best solution is maybe just to add the workaround given above to the documentation, perhaps in the `Plotting_with_Matplotlib` notebook. 



I am here to confirm that this issue still exists to this date.
Same issue here...

problem_statement: Timezone-aware data is plotted incorrectly when using `rasterize` (plotted as though axis were UTC) #### ALL software version info ```python import sys import holoviews as hv import pandas as pd import datashader import bokeh print(f"{sys.version=}\n{hv.__version__=}\n{pd.__version__=}\n{datashader.__version__=}\n{bokeh.__version__=}") ``` ``` sys.version='3.8.10 | packaged by conda-forge | (default, May 10 2021, 22:58:09) \n[Clang 11.1.0 ]' hv.__version__='1.14.6' pd.__version__='1.2.5' datashader.__version__='0.13.0' bokeh.__version__='2.4.0' ``` #### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior When plotting using `rasterize`, timezone-aware data is plotted incorrectly on the axes. (The plot incorrectly treats the x-axis as though it were UTC-local, and the plot appears blank when initially rendered.) Somewhat related to this old bug https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/issues/4526, but specific to `rasterize`. #### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue When not using rasterize: ```python import holoviews as hv import pandas as pd t = pd.date_range(start='2020-01-01 10:00', end='2020-01-01 12:00', freq='H', tz='Asia/Shanghai') d = pd.DataFrame({'t': t, 'y': [1, 2, 3]}) curve = hv.Curve(d, kdims=['t'], vdims=['y']) curve ``` it produces this correct graph as expected (local time on the x-axis): When using `rasterize`, it still shows the x-axis as 10:00 to 12:00 (same as above). **However**, the data is incorrectly plotted as though the x-axis were UTC time (even though in this case it's actually Asia/Shanghai), and so the chart appears blank at first: ```python from holoviews.operation.datashader import rasterize rasterize(curve) ``` If we zoom out the x-axis, we find that the line was incorrectly drawn at 02:00-04:00 (rather than 10-12), which is the UTC-converted version of the timestamps (and not the correct Asia/Shanghai time): Thank you! Really appreciate the great tools you've all built with holoviews, datashader, etc.
hints_text:
Thanks for reporting this!

Given that the plots works fine without rasterize, I strongly suspect the issue is with datashader's handling of these pandas `Timestamp`s (and their timezones). I'll transfer this issue to the datashader repo for now (and it can be transferred back if it really is HoloView's that is at fault!)

@maximlt has helpfully offered to investigate!
Datashader doesn't do much (anything?) with times itself, so it can also be HoloViews' conversion to things Datashader understands...

index b7b6764eca..0cc9a33792 100644
--- a/holoviews/plotting/plotly/element.py
+++ b/holoviews/plotting/plotly/element.py
@@ -13,7 +13,7 @@
 from ...streams import Stream
 from ...util.transform import dim
 from ..plot import GenericElementPlot, GenericOverlayPlot
-from ..util import dim_range_key
+from ..util import _NAMED_CSS_COLORS, dim_range_key
 from .plot import PlotlyPlot
 from .util import (
     PLOTLY_MAP,
@@ -386,11 +386,39 @@ def _apply_transforms(self, element, ranges, style):
                                      'to overlay your data along the dimension.')
 
             # If color is not valid colorspec add colormapper
-            numeric = isinstance(val, np.ndarray) and dtype_kind(val) in 'uifMm'
-            if ('color' in k and isinstance(val, np.ndarray) and numeric):
-                copts = self.get_color_opts(v, element, ranges, style)
-                new_style.pop('cmap', None)
-                new_style.update(copts)
+            if 'color' in k and isinstance(val, util.arraylike_types):
+                if dtype_kind(val) in 'uifMm':  # numeric
+                    copts = self.get_color_opts(v, element, ranges, style)
+                    new_style.pop('cmap', None)
+                    new_style.update(copts)
+                else:  # categorical
+                    categories, cat_indices = np.unique(val, return_inverse=True)
+                    is_plotly_allowed_colors = all(
+                        isinstance(c, str) and (
+                            c.startswith(('#', 'rgb', 'hsl', 'hsv')) or c in _NAMED_CSS_COLORS
+                        ) for c in categories
+                    )
+
+                    if not is_plotly_allowed_colors:
+                        val = cat_indices
+
+                        copts = self.get_color_opts(v, element, ranges, style)
+                        copts.update({
+                            'cmin': 0,
+                            'cmax': len(categories) - 1,
+                            'cauto': False
+                        })
+
+                        if 'colorbar' in copts:
+                            copts['colorbar'].update({
+                                'tickmode': 'array',
+                                'tickvals': list(range(len(categories))),
+                                'ticktext': [v.dimension.pprint_value(c) for c in categories]
+                            })
+
+                        new_style.pop('cmap', None)
+                        new_style.update(copts)
+
             new_style[k] = val
         return new_style

problem_statement:
Colorizing the points of a Scatter plot with the Plotly backend raises an error
Colorizing the points of a Scatter plot with the Plotly backend using `.opts(color=dim_name)` raises an error.

```python
import holoviews as hv
import pandas as pd

hv.extension('plotly')

df = pd.DataFrame([[1, 2, 'A', 0.1], [3, 4, 'B', 0.2], [5, 6, 'C', 0.3]],
                            columns=['x', 'y', 'category', 'number'])

hv.Scatter(df, kdims=['x'], vdims=['y', 'category']).opts(color='category')
```

Just checking which of my issues are still valid.

This is still a valid issue. 
As we use DynamicMap here, the plot will just be updated inplace, not replaced entirely. That means, the plot and its axes information only get initialized once. Adding/updating `subcoordinate_y` later won't make any effects. Something likes this should work:

```python
SUBCOORD_Y = True

def return_overlay(run):
    if not run:
        return (hv.Curve([], label='1').opts(subcoordinate_y=SUBCOORD_Y) * hv.Curve([], label='2').opts(subcoordinate_y=SUBCOORD_Y))
    return (hv.Curve([0,1], label='1') * hv.Curve([1,0], label='2'))

run_button = pn.widgets.Button(name='run')
pn.Column(hv.DynamicMap(pn.bind(return_overlay, run=run_button)), run_button)
```

Discussed with @hoxbro and we agreed that the KeyError about `subcoordinate_y` is quite confusing. Will make it a warning with clearer messages.
While writing tests for this, we realized it's not about axes not updated. It's about the labels not being set for initial plots. Label mismatching between initial and updated plots causes the error.

problem_statement:
The pointerXY stream breaks on heatmaps with categorical kdims
#### ALL software version info
python 3.10
bokeh              3.4.1
holoviews          1.18.3
panel              1.4.2
ubuntu 22.04
chrome 124.0
notebook: N/A running as a python script with panel

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior
Moving the mouse from inside to outside the heatmap with categorical axes should not trigger an exception.

I set up a PointerXY stream on a heatmap so I can run a callback on the value the pointer is hovering over. If I have a heatmap with integer or float axes, everything works as expected.  If one of my axes has categorical values and I move across the top edge of the heatmap I get an IndexError exception for holoviews. 

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
import pandas as pd
import holoviews as hv
import panel as pn

hv.extension("bokeh")

# create 2x2 dataset with integer keys for key1 and key2
df_integer = pd.DataFrame(
    ([[0, 0, 10], [0, 1, 20], [1, 0, 20], [1, 1, 30]]), columns=["key1", "key2", "value"]
)

# create 2x2 dataset with an integer key for key1 and a string key for key2
df_categorical = pd.DataFrame(
    ([[0, "A", 10], [0, "B", 20], [1, "A", 20], [1, "B", 30]]), columns=["key1", "key2", "value"]
)

def move_pointer(x, y):
    print(x, y)

def create_heatmap_streamxy(df: pd.DataFrame):
    """Take a dataframe and return a heatmap that has a streamxy callback."""
    ds = hv.Dataset(df)

    heatmap = ds.to(hv.HeatMap, kdims=["key1", "key2"], vdims="value").opts(tools=["hover"])
    hv.streams.PointerXY(source=heatmap).add_subscriber(move_pointer)
    return heatmap


row = pn.Row()
row.append(create_heatmap_streamxy(df_integer))
row.append(create_heatmap_streamxy(df_categorical))
row.show()
```

Improve support for non-numpy dtypes
We should improve the support for different dtypes; currently, we really only support numpy. 

dtypes which make sense to support:
- Pandas dtype, like  [`pd.Int64dtype`](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/reference/api/pandas.Int64Dtype.html)

problem_statement:
x marker rendered as + in legend
#### ALL software version info

```
holoviews==1.19.1
bokeh==3.5.1
```

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

When overlaying scatter plots with `x` markers, the marker is rendered as a `+` in the legend instead of a `x`.

#### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue

```
import holoviews as hv

hv.extension('bokeh')

hv.Scatter([(1, 1)]).opts(marker='cross',size=10).relabel('cross') * hv.Scatter([(1,2)]).opts(marker='x', size=10).relabel('x')
```
 
#### Screenshots or screencasts of the bug in action

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e921f78f-2bd1-4369-a9b7-0d6cd6fc632a)
all_hints_text: I encountered this bug recently when I needed a plot using the specific markers "+" and "x", and, as above, the legend showed "+" for both. The linked pull request briefly describes the cause and should resolve the issue. I noticed that an issue related to this one is #3514 (now locked due to inactivity), where the triangle markers all point upward in the legend. They do so because, as in this issue, *Holoviews* translates each named *matplotlib* marker into a *Bokeh* marker with the appropriate angle for plotting, but *Bokeh* draws all such markers in the legend with the same angle to show its base marker. With the above PR applied, *matplotlib*'s triangle marker names are the only remaining ones for which *Holoviews* assigns an angle when translating them to *Bokeh* equivalents: ```python import bokeh import holoviews as hv hv.extension("bokeh") hv.plotting.bokeh.styles.markers ``` > ``` > {'+': {'marker': 'cross'}, > 's': {'marker': 'square'}, > 'd': {'marker': 'diamond'}, > '^': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': 0}, > '>': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': -1.5707963267948966}, > 'v': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': 3.141592653589793}, > '<': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': 1.5707963267948966}, > '1': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': 0}, > '2': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': -1.5707963267948966}, > '3': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': 3.141592653589793}, > '4': {'marker': 'triangle', 'angle': 1.5707963267948966}, > 'o': {'marker': 'circle'}, > '*': {'marker': 'asterisk'}} > ``` The remaining differences in marker behavior with *Bokeh* can be seen in the following, where we plot each marker at a 30° angle: ```python # Merge Bokeh and matplotlib marker names with a simple ordered set to handle both pre- # and post-PR-6676. markers = list( { marker: None for markers in (bokeh.core.enums.MarkerType, hv.plotting.bokeh.styles.markers) for marker in markers } ) marker_count = len(markers) row_count = 7 col_count = (marker_count - 1) // row_count + 1 hv.Overlay( [ hv.Points([divmod(index, row_count)], label=marker).opts( hv.opts.Points(marker=marker) ) for index, marker in enumerate(markers) ] ).opts( hv.opts.Points(size=16, fill_color=None, angle=30), hv.opts.Overlay( invert_yaxis=True, frame_width=780, frame_height=300, legend_position="top", legend_limit=marker_count, legend_cols=col_count, legend_opts=dict(glyph_height=40, glyph_width=40, spacing=0, label_width=100), ), ) ``` The first four columns are *Bokeh*'s native markers, and the last two are translated *matplotlib* markers. There are two groups of behaviors: * For *Bokeh*'s native markers and *matplotlib*'s non-triangle markers ("+", "s", "d", "o", and "*"), the plot respects the requested angle, while the legend shows the symbol suggested by each marker's name at a particular reference angle. (However, the markers with rotational symmetry have no apparent angle.) * For *matplotlib*'s triangle markers ("^", ">", "v", "<", "1", "2", "3", and "4"), the plot ignores the requested angle and instead draws the named symbol at a fixed angle, while the legend shows the base *Bokeh* marker at a reference angle (which is an upward triangle and not necessarily the named symbol). Before PR #6676, the "x" marker was the only native *Bokeh* marker that behaved like *matplotlib*'s triangle markers, in that the plot ignored any requested angle while the legend showed the base "+" shape from which *Holoviews* derived the "x". Plots with *Bokeh* that need upward and downward triangles in the legend could use the markers "triangle" and "inverted_triangle", respectively. This issue has been automatically locked since there has not been any recent activity after it was closed. Please open a new issue for related bugs.

problem_statement:
Server side HoverTool follow ups
### HoloViews:

- [x] See https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/pull/6422#discussion_r1957970505
https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/blob/48f91a59cb961092e9c60ef068a54600e213645b/holoviews/operation/datashader.py#L1763-L1766

- [x] Add documentation explaining the difference between selector and aggregator, e.g., that selector is a subset of aggregator, basically that you select a row (`min`, `max`, `first`, `last`, etc.) and not "combine" multiple rows (`mean`, `std`, etc.) 
- [x] Improve information in hover tool for selector https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/pull/6568
- [x] Make it possible to rename HoverTool columns.  https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/pull/6568


### Bokeh (as of Bokeh 3.6, not yet supported in Bokeh)

- [x]  https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/14123
    Currently, we do a simple conversion
- [ ] https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/9087
    Currently, we show the Hovertool even when no data is available, resulting in a HoverTool filled with nans.
